source.cross_reference: JP17 (parent catalog entry). This sample targets the astro-tool adapter subsystem specifically.

full_prompt:
Design a passive tool adapter that mounts to an Astrobee-class free-flying robot aboard the ISS Japanese Experiment Module for an ISS microgravity tool-adapter benchmark. The adapter holds a set of AR-tag target cards at fixed relative poses so student-coded Astrobee missions can navigate, locate the cards, and point a laser at specific sub-targets. The adapter is launched inside a Cargo Transfer Bag (CTB), installed by the astronaut, and attached to Astrobee via the standard perching-arm end-effector interface.

Geometry. The adapter is a rectangular frame 220 mm wide x 160 mm tall x 30 mm deep, machined from Delrin (acetal homopolymer) for ISS flammability and outgassing compliance. The front face carries six AR-tag pockets arranged in a 3 x 2 grid, each 40 mm x 40 mm with a 3 mm recess to seat a laminated card. The back face has the Astrobee perching-arm interface: a standard 25 mm diameter stainless-steel grapple post with a 19 mm shoulder, bonded and pinned to the frame. The frame has four 4 mm M4 captive-nut locations for emergency astronaut hand-install should the auto-perch fail. Maximum wet mass (including AR cards) 350 g.

Material. Delrin 150 acetal homopolymer (Young's modulus 3.1 GPa, density 1420 kg/m^3, yield strength 72 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 89 MPa, glass transition -60 C, flammability compliant with NASA-STD-6001B Test 1). The grapple post is 316L stainless steel (Young's modulus 193 GPa, density 8000 kg/m^3, yield 205 MPa). Analyses at 22 C nominal ISS cabin temperature, but thermal excursion 18-27 C must be considered for CTE mismatch at the grapple bond.

Load cases. (LC1) Launch vibration in CTB (Dragon cargo): random vibration 20-2000 Hz at 6.8 g-rms per NASA-STD-5002 CTB spec, applied in all three axes. (LC2) Astrobee perch pull-off: 8 N axial pull through the grapple post simulating an abort retraction. (LC3) Astronaut hand-install shear: 15 N shear load on any single M4 captive nut simulating off-axis insertion. (LC4) Worst-case contingency impact: Astrobee colliding with the adapter frame at 0.1 m/s with Astrobee's 9.5 kg mass producing a ~10 N quasi-static contact at the AR-card face.

Pass/fail criteria. (R1) Maximum von Mises stress in the Delrin frame must not exceed 36 MPa (yield / 2.0 for polymer safety factor) under LC1, LC2, LC3, and LC4. (R2) The grapple-post bond joint must transfer 8 N pull-off without adhesive failure, demonstrated via a worst-case shear stress in the bond line below 2 MPa. (R3) First natural frequency must be above 150 Hz to stay above random-vibration energy peak at 80 Hz per NASA-STD-5002. (R4) Total wet mass including six AR cards must not exceed 350 g. (R5) All wetted surfaces must pass NASA-STD-6001B Test 1 flammability (demonstrated by material compliance certificate, not FEA).

Solver and analysis expectations. Primary verification is linear static structural FEA for LC2, LC3, LC4. Random-vibration analysis via PSD input for LC1 (the CTB 6.8 g-rms spectrum). Modal analysis for R3. A CTE-mismatch thermal stress check at the Delrin-316L bond is required but is a linear thermal-structural coupled analysis with a 5 C delta, not a full thermal network. Fluid analysis not required (microgravity, no convection). Radiation analysis not applicable inside the ISS pressurized volume.

Deliverables. STEP (AP242) + Parasolid for both the Delrin frame and the stainless grapple post as separate parts plus an assembly. Report covering LC1-LC4 stress extrema, first 5 natural frequencies, bond-line shear, CTE-mismatch peak stress, and a material compliance declaration referencing NASA-STD-6001B Test 1.
short_prompt:
Design a passive tool adapter that mounts to an Astrobee-class free-flying robot aboard the ISS JEM, holding six AR-tag target cards for student-coded navigation / laser-pointing missions. The adapter is a Delrin 150 acetal rectangular frame, 220 mm wide x 160 mm tall x 30 mm deep, with a 3 x 2 grid of 40 x 40 mm AR-tag pockets (3 mm recess) on the front face and a 25 mm dia x 19 mm shoulder 316L stainless grapple post on the back bonded-and-pinned into the frame, plus four M4 captive-nut emergency hand-install locations. Wet mass with AR cards <=350 g. The adapter is stowed inside a CTB and installed by the astronaut, then attached to Astrobee via the standard perching-arm interface.

Governing standards: NASA-STD-5002 (CTB random-vibration levels) and NASA-STD-6001B (flammability / offgassing of wetted surfaces).

Load cases.
- LC1 CTB random vibration: 6.8 GRMS, 20-2000 Hz, each axis.
- LC2 Astrobee perch pull-off: 8 N axial through the grapple post.
- LC3 astronaut hand-install shear: 15 N on any single M4 captive nut.
- LC4 Astrobee collision: 10 N quasi-static at the AR-card face (9.5 kg at 0.1 m/s).

Also check CTE mismatch at the Delrin-316L bond over the 18-27 C ISS cabin thermal excursion.

prompt.title: Astrobee-class AR-tag tool adapter (ISS microgravity benchmark)

geometric_constraints.grapple_post: 25 mm dia x 19 mm shoulder, 316L stainless

load_cases.name: astronaut_install_shear
load_cases.axis_orientation: shear on single M4 captive nut

exclusions_explained.fluid_analysis: Microgravity, no natural convection; ISS ventilation modeled separately.
exclusions_explained.radiation_analysis: Pressurized ISS volume; adapter lifetime <1 mission.

notes.cross_reference: Parent catalog entry JP17 covers the overall competition. This sample targets the AR-tag tool adapter as a distinct structural deliverable.
